This is another one of those gemstones of the entire event that I hope you guys take from this, because you're not always gonna have 90 minutes to sell your thing to someone. So this is how to condense that, still change someone's beliefs and the way they see the world and offer something at a high ticket price. Make sense? Awesome, so please with me, please stand and rise and welcome to the stage, Mr. Russell Brunson. How you guys feeling? Woo! Who's a little tired? No! Nobody? Well, I'm excited to be here. This part is fun. Okay, Stephen probably told you some of the back story about how this whole thing happened, but I'm gonna tell you my words because it helps set this up better. So, the perfect webinar hack, the way this whole concept kind of came about was, I was supposed to be promoting something for Mike Phil same, who's one of my closest friends, been in this business together forever, and he wanted me to promote this thing. I was like, yeah, I'm gonna do this big promotion for him and write a webinar, we're gonna sell a bunch of things, because he was selling webinar software, he's back in the webinar jam, first came out, and he didn't have a webinar to sell webinar software, which is like the stupidest idea in the world. And so I was like, well, I'm gonna write a webinar to sell your software and I'll make a bunch of money, and I ran all the way from here to there. Woo, I'm gonna catch my breath. I'm out of shape. Anyway, so I told him I was gonna do a webinar and then I was going to, I was going to, I was going to, I never did, and then this launch was down to like last two or three days before launch was gonna end, and I'm like, I don't have time to write a webinar. And he's like, Brunson, when are you gonna promote, man? You gotta promote, you gotta promote. I'm like, I don't have time to do a webinar. And he's like, just promote it. I'm like, no, your page doesn't convert, it's horrible. No one's ever gonna buy this thing no matter what. And he's like, well, dude, the top prize is a BMW. I'm like, I don't need, I'm not a car guy. I don't need a car. He's like, well, if you don't want a BMW, you can give you cash, you can give you wherever you want. And by the time I had my eye on a Cryoson, have you guys ever been in a Cryoson before? I was like, I really want a Cryoson, but if I was to tell my wife I'm gonna buy a Cryoson, she would've thought I was insane. So it's a big, huge gas chamber that you step inside. It's a big liquid nitrogen tank, you click a button. Oh, by the way, you're naked in there, except for your, anyway, and it fills the thing up full of liquid nitrogen gas and it takes your body temperature down. It's like negative 240 degrees and you stand this thing, it freezes you for three minutes and you get out, right? So I want one really bad, but if I was like, come home with that, my wife would've been so mad. So I'm like, okay, if I win it though, she can't like, not let me have it. So I was like, Mike, this is the deal. If I win, can you buy me a Cryoson instead of a BMW? He's like, what are you, what's a Cryoson? I sent a picture of it. He's like, you are so weird, whatever you want though, I will do it. I'm like, okay, now I got a goal. Okay, so I was like, we have three days first launches over, I need to write a webinar. I'm like, I have no time for a webinar, what should I do? And I was like, let's just do a webinar. And so I got my marker out on our whiteboard and I was like, what's his offer? And I'm like, it wasn't my own product, but he was, I'm his affiliate first product. And just like I talked, we talked about a lot of last two days. Like he was selling webinar software, transactional, right? It's like one thing. I was like, if I'm gonna be competing with all the other fillets, I can't just sell like the software, okay? Differentially, I was making a little money, a lot of money, is like understanding all this offer. Like how to create good offers. Like, what is a good offer? Everyone else is selling software. I wanna create, I wanna sell something a million times better than software. That way I can get people who have already bought the software to buy it again because my offer's so good, which happened a lot. In fact, we've had people buy our products multiple times because the offer shifts a little bit and they want the new stuff and they buy again and again. So learning how to create offers is really important. So I sat there and I was like, okay, well, Mike's software's what they're buying and then what am I gonna give them? They can get, oh, thank you. They can get this and this. So I made this offer over here, right? So they're gonna get my Perfect Webinar Script, my Perfect Webinar Training. They're gonna give me a video of me actually doing it live, the Perfect Webinar funnel, my funnel. So I like listed down and created an offer that they would get for free. So I'm gonna use free again. You can always get for free when you buy Mike's software. So now I have the sexy software out of everyone in the fillet contest and I was like, okay, what am I? Like what's my hook? I was gonna give them to actually watch my Facebook live. So I was like, how to make boomer out of the headline? And Mike, what are the false beliefs? False belief, false belief, false belief. And then at the time, this was two Christmases ago. I thought it'd be really fun if all of our employees, instead of sending like a Christmas bonus, like a check in the mail, I was gonna be more fun if we gave them a backpack and fill the full $1 bills. That was a really good idea that turned out to be a nightmare for Melanie. She spent two months taking $1 bills. First, like you can't go to the bank and request like $40,000 in ones. Did you know that? They don't have the money in the bank. I was like, I thought you're a bank. There's a big vault. They had nothings in there. Like what? So like she had to go for like months around bank to bank to bank, getting all the $1 bills they had till she had enough $1 bills for the entire company. And she spent three months like, she's like, can I just put them in there? Like no, like I want them to open it and they pop out, right? And so she's rolling these dollar bills and like shoving them in. It took her like literally two months. This is like the first three months she worked for me. I swear I'm probably the weirdest boss she's ever had. Right? Okay. And so we had all these things. It's so like we're doing this and I'm about to do the webinar and I was like, how do I make this exciting? And then there was all these stacks of $1 bills in our office. So I'm like, I'm gonna do that thing that like the gangster rappers do on the thing where they make it money fly. So I like get all these stacks of $1 bills. And Brent had two phones, one with Facebook Live and one with Periscope. And we're like, hey, let's just see what we can do. And so they clicked record, record. And I got stacks of cash. I'm like, look, they do the whole thing and making it rain, having fun. Just to like get people's attention while they're showing up. And I was like, okay, here we go. And I went and I just did this. And I went through one thing, three secrets, did the offer, closed the sale. And then for the next three days, we drove ads to that Facebook Live and ended up getting like, I can't remember, 400 or 500,000 people watch that video in that three or four day winter period time. We destroyed every one of the affiliate contests and I got my very own cryos on it in my house. So awesome. So now the cooler part of the story, exact story is cool, but the cooler one is that Brandon and Katelyn who are very good at just implementing everything the second we do it. Like whenever they're at these events, it's so funny because Katelyn's like listening and Brandon's typing and like the time the person's off stage that I can't get out of life. And like what the thing you just talked about, like how did you do that that fast? Like they're the fastest implementers I've ever met. And so literally while I'm doing this, I didn't know this till a year later, they told me while I'm doing this, Katelyn had been doing her webinar every single week for up to this point and she was like, I know what he's doing. He's doing the perfect webinar. Oh my gosh, we should do this. And they had already done the perfect webinar a million times. So she got her whiteboard out, she wrote the things, with our stack, I have little pieces of paper taped over it and I like pulled off each thing. So you're like, oh, you get the reveal, you know? So they were doing the same thing about an hour after mine ended, they clicked record on theirs and they did the exact same thing that they're Facebook live and crushed it with theirs. And since then I've probably done this a dozen times or so and every time we do it just makes, it's awesome and it's fast. And so this is the point I wanted all you guys to get to where you understand the process so well, where you're like, I don't need a hook, I don't need three secrets to destroy false belief here and here, I need to get an offer. And that's really all you need to sell and match and you can do it really, really fast. So that's kind of the goal where we're going. Okay, so that's the family business. Doing the lawn mowers, doing the irrigation, all kinds of stuff. And then you got back in computer science. Yeah, yeah, yeah. Background in computer science. I can tell you why I got into this world. And this doesn't really apply to our customer, but about two years ago, two and a half years ago, this Jacobson manufacturer, they're about 80% of our revenue. We've got around 40 employees. I think we've got like three, four, five buildings, 20 something acres. It's not as big as a lot of people's companies. There's just a lot of stuff moving with our parent company. Two years ago, we thought the lawn was gonna be taken away from us. So myself, my wife, my mom, my dad, our director of sales, kind of the management team, we were the only ones that knew this. We spent the next like 48 hours going through how in the world can we keep these doors open and came up to if this happened, the doors were gonna have to shut. We were gonna have to have 40 families that, sorry guys, you don't know where the check's gonna come from. So fortunately for us, that didn't happen. We maintained the exclusive rights. And I looked at my parents and I said, this will never, ever happen again. We will never be in this position to where somebody holds the power over us. So I started figuring out how can I do that? Well, process, software, knowing the golf market, this is what I've come up with, is the software. So the last two years I've been building it, you guys have seen me limp and I've told my story quite a bit, but shattered my, like obliterated my ankle on a trampoline with my then three year old. So I've been building the software for two years. I've had four surgeries, two surgeons actually, after the third surgery didn't work, recommended amputation, ended up with a surgeon in Charlotte that does a lot of the Carolina Panthers work. He's fixed my leg, it's still not perfect, but at least I saved my leg. My wife has gone through a pregnancy, a delivery and raising a 15 month old that's never seen me walk. I've walked, I put on 40 pounds, started walking about three or four months ago over the last two years. So my entrepreneurial journey that I thought was gonna be easy, like a lot of life smacks in the face. Every time I feel like I get to the top of the mountain, I look, it's just another cliff, right? And I still have a lot more rock to climb, but do feel like we're getting close. So you jump on a trampoline, how, tell me about that. So I don't know, I mean, at that point I was 200 pounds, I was in pretty good shape, and my three year old fell underneath me as I was up in the air, and I just kind of twisted out of the way so I didn't land on him, and then just exploded. Doctor said it was like the equivalent of a 75 mile an hour head on collision. If they were to empty it, would it be? It was gonna be beneath the knee. I'd already, like I had already come to terms with amputation, I was so tired of not being able to walk, not being able to be a father, I had to stop coaching, had to basically be taken care of, which I don't think any of us entrepreneurs like to be taken care of. We like to do the taken care of. And so I was ready to do it, but I wanted to have a last couple of opinions, and they finally found somebody said they could fix it, and they fixed it. Dang, that's cool. All right, so, tell me about your friend yesterday, you were telling me about your friend, they got you into the golf, or they got you into the golf. Yeah, so the Chuck story's true, his name's actually Chuck. The sequence of events didn't happen the way my origin story was written yesterday, but in the 90s, there's big boom in golf construction, golf course is being built left and right. We had just economic supply, demand, oversupply, but the demand was still there, so. I think this Tiger Woods was awesome, everyone be Tiger Woods. Dude, you'd be, you know what? Yeah, I mean that has a lot to do with it. So then 2008 happened, Tiger Woods happened, all these different things happened that just made golf start to constrict. I was having a conversation with, I'm not sure, anyways, today with somebody about, you probably don't play golf, I don't play golf, I bet the majority of us in here don't play golf. Who plays golf? Are you memberships, so that's less than like a quarter of the room, you're probably public, semi-private play, you're probably not a member, right? You're not giving that capital contribution, you're not maintaining that monthly minimum, so revenues, capital expenditures, everything has shrunk for golf, and so golf is a shrinking economy. Our customer, Chuck, went to school to learn the craft of grass, how to make that disease go away, how to make it look as healthy and as green as possible, but there was a pretty major transition in the golf market to where budgets were starting to be tied, and Chuck used to have all the budget that he ever needed to do whatever he wanted. He was a rock star at his course. Members loved him, he went out eating, drinking with the members, playing with the members, then when they started shrinking his budget, conditions started going down, Chuck started feeling the heat, Chuck knew how to grow grass, he didn't know how to run the business of what I call the business, he understood the craft, not the business, of running a $2 million budget. So we saw this, so this is kind of where the timeline changes a little bit with what actually happened, we saw this was a problem, we actually tried to find some software to help the literal Chuck out, and then all the other Chucks that are in the industry. And the fact of the matter is it didn't exist. There was no software out there for this kind of new day and age of the golf economy. So fast forward, a year and a half ago, we're building the software Chuck gets fired from his, where he was a rock star, he's gone. Family's upset, uprooted, they're looking at moving halfway around the country for like a 50% pay decrease, just it's not good and Chuck's not the only story of this happening. And so we're developing this software, and we give the software to Chuck and some other Chucks and it's not good. So I never had proper training in like running a software development team. So I've spent the last two years and amongst all the other stuff, kind of getting what we call my MBA and software development and like running a software team. So through this process, we realized that it's a lot more than just the software that Chuck needed. My favorite kind of like bridge is it's kind of like giving Chuck an easel and a paintbrush, but never teaching him how to paint. So the software is the easel and the paintbrush. So on my way out here, it was an epiphany I had a week ago. On my way out here, we started building out, okay, what does this class look like to teach Chuck how to paint? So that's kind of where we are right now. Where I've got my staff is in the process of building out all these assets. We're gonna do a master class for Chuck and all the Chucks out there. And then our software is just a tool to implement that. Is it marketing software or is it like? No, it's mainly, so this being a business owner, I guess we all do, our biggest expense is labor, is payroll. That is the same for golf as well. Chuck's responsible for a $2 million budget and labor, equipment, chemical and fertilizer, which are the three biggest- Labor, equipment, fertilizer? Labor, equipment, and then the chemicals and fertilizers to keep the grass healthy are the three biggest line items for a golf course's overall budget. So maintenance is the biggest line item for a golf course's budget. And then in the maintenance budget, those are the three big ones. So Chuck is not very good at managing people. Chuck is not very good at managing processes. So our system, we call it plan, execute report. So we help Chuck craft a plan for all these areas for his staff, his equipment, his tasks, his chemicals, his fertilizers, and his budget. And then once that's in our system, our system's now smart enough to know and help Chuck execute what needs to happen. Is it just for Chuck or for anybody working on a golf course? So it's for the entire maintenance of the golf course? So you know. So was Chuck in there, he'd be the maintenance guy of the golf course? Yeah, Chuck's the, and he's this golf course superintendent in charge of maintenance. Now, so our true customer is actually the owner or whoever's pocket it's gonna affect, but we feel like our software and our process has to be taught to the guy that's gonna use it and make it better. And we were able to reduce our service shop overhead by $200,000, like tangible, I can prove it by monitoring these two metrics across our service staff. And so we're gonna teach how that happens. What these metrics are, how you measure them. And then inside of turf assistant, our tool, we have, it's called time assistant. So that kind of sucks. But you're done with them? Yes. How much did they save? We have, so I haven't done those numbers because I can't promote them. What do you think you need? Well, so the superintendent used to spend from three o'clock just himself, not his staff, just himself, from three o'clock until five o'clock, planning for the next day. Now just himself, he spends 30 minutes using our software. So that's not the time study side, but that's just the automation, execution piece of our plan XQ report. So it's worth 20K. 20,000 dollars worth, 20,000 dollars, right? Yeah. Okay. Dun, dun, dun. Okay. Anybody else in business owner wants? The dude giving you money, he wants something out of this too besides just the other guy's doing something like he wants, like feel good about this from, I'll tell you what else we can give him. Do you think he's gonna be, do you think the person watching the webinar, it's probably also gonna be the owner? No, it's probably Chuck. Because Chuck, he's like, he's like, pitch the owner. Because at the end of the day, Chuck knows that there's a problem. Like he is problem aware. He's not solution aware. He knows he has a problem. He cannot justify to his members in management, either A, why he needs more budget or B, why the course conditions are the way they are. So is his goal to make more efficiency, he makes money in his pocket or is his goal to make? No, his goal is to keep his job.
